sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me check the current eye positions. Looking at the image, the eyes appear to be at:
- Left eye: rows 49-53, cols 19-23 (initial left position!)
- Right eye: rows 44-48, cols 29-33 (moved left from 34-38)
So the left eye is now at the initial position (rows 49-53, cols 19-23), but the right eye is at rows 44-48, cols 29-33 (not the initial right position of rows 49-53, cols 39-43).
To get the right eye to the initial position, I need to move it DOWN by 1 (from rows 44-48 to 49-53) and RIGHT by 2 (from cols 29-33 to 39-43).
But when I press DOWN, both eyes move down. The left eye would move from rows 49-53 to 54-58, and the right eye would move from rows 44-48 to 49-53.
Then I need to press UP to move the left eye back up to rows 49-53. But this would also move the right eye up from rows 49-53 to 44-48.
This is a problem. The eyes seem to move together, so I can't independently control them.
Wait, but maybe the eyes don't always move together. Let me check if the left eye is blocked at rows 54-58 (bottom). If the left eye is at the bottom, pressing DOWN might not move it further down. Similarly, if the right eye is at the top, pressing UP might not move it further up.
Let me try pressing DOWN to move both eyes down. If the left eye is blocked at the bottom, it stays at rows 49-53. If the right eye is not blocked, it moves to rows 49-53.
Actually, looking at the image, the left eye is at rows 49-53, which is not the bottom (rows 54-58). So pressing DOWN would move the left eye to rows 54-58.
Let me try a different approach. What if I press RIGHT first to move the right eye to cols 39-43, and then press DOWN to move the right eye to rows 49-53?
Currently:
- Left eye: rows 49-53, cols 19-23
- Right eye: rows 44-48, cols 29-33
If I press RIGHT:
- Left eye: rows 49-53, cols 24-28
- Right eye: rows 44-48, cols 34-38
If I press RIGHT again:
- Left eye: rows 49-53, cols 29-33
- Right eye: rows 44-48, cols 39-43 (initial right column!)
Then if I press DOWN:
- Left eye: rows 54-58, cols 29-33
- Right eye: rows 49-53, cols 39-43 (initial right position!)
Then if I press LEFT:
- Left eye: rows 54-58, cols 24-28
- Right eye: rows 49-53, cols 34-38
Hmm, this doesn't get me to the initial position either.
Actually, I realize I need to think about this differently. The eyes move together when I press arrow keys. So to get both eyes to the initial position (rows 49-53, cols 19-23 and 39-43), I need to find a sequence of moves that gets both eyes there.
The initial position has both eyes at rows 49-53. The current position has the left eye at rows 49-53 and the right eye at rows 44-48. To get the right eye to rows 49-53, I need to press DOWN. But this would also move the left eye to rows 54-58.
Wait, but what if the left eye is blocked at rows 54-58? Let me check the face shape. The left eye is in the yellow half of the face, which extends from rows 9-58. So the left eye can move from rows 9-58. But the eye is a 5x5 block, so the minimum row is 9 and the maximum row is 58-5+1=54. So the left eye can be at rows 9-13, 14-18, ..., 49-53, 54-58.
Actually, looking at the boundary, the face extends from rows 9-58. The eye is a 5x5 block. So the eye can be at rows 9-13, 14-18, 19-23, 24-28, 29-33, 34-38, 39-43, 44-48, 49-53, 54-58. That's 10 possible row positions.
Similarly, the face extends from cols 9-53. The left eye can be at cols 9-13, 14-18, 19-23, 24-28, 29-33 (within the yellow half). The right eye can be at cols 29-33, 34-38, 39-43, 44-48, 49-53 (within the orange half).
Wait, but the eyes might be constrained to specific positions within their halves. Let me check the initial position: left eye at cols 19-23, right eye at cols 39-43. The face extends from cols 9-53, which is 45 columns. The left half is cols 9-31 (23 columns) and the right half is cols 32-53 (22 columns). But the eyes are 5x5 blocks, so they can be at cols 9-13, 14-18, 19-23, 24-28, 29-33 in the left half, and cols 29-33, 34-38, 39-43, 44-48, 49-53 in the right half.
Actually, I think the eyes are constrained by the black face outline. The left eye can only move within the yellow region inside the face, and the right eye can only move within the orange region inside the face. The face outline might limit the eye positions.
Let me just try to get the eyes to the initial position and see if SPACE works there. I'll try pressing DOWN to move the right eye down, then LEFT to move the left eye left, then UP to move the right eye up... No, this is getting too complicated.
Let me try a different approach. What if the goal is not about the initial position at all? What if the goal is about some other position?
Actually, I've been spending too many actions on this. Let me try a completely different hypothesis: what if the goal is to make the eyes "look" at each other by having them at the center position (cols 24-28 and 34-38)? Or what if the goal is to have the eyes at the top position (rows 39-43)?
Let me try pressing UP to move the eyes up, then SPACE.

# Currently left is at 49-53, right is at 44-48.

# If I press UP: left=(49-53, 19-23), right=(44-48, 29-33). Back to start.
# It seems like the eyes are always at different rows when they're at different columns.
# Let me check: can the eyes be at the same row AND same column? No, they'd overlap.
# Can the eyes be at the same row but different columns? Yes, the initial position has this.
# So the question is: how do I get both eyes to rows 49-53?
# Currently: left=(49-53, 19-23), right=(44-48, 29-33)
# The right eye is at rows 44-48, which is one row above 49-53.
# If I press DOWN, right moves to 49-53 but left moves to 54-58.
# If I then press UP, left moves to 49-53 but right moves to 44-48.
# This is a cycle. The eyes can't both be at 49-53 unless they're at the initial columns.
# Let me try: RIGHT, RIGHT to get right eye to cols 39-43
# After RIGHT: left=(49-53, 24-28), right=(44-48, 34-38)
# After RIGHT: left=(49-53, 29-33), right=(44-48, 39-43)
# Now right eye is at cols 39-43 (initial right column)
# If I press DOWN: left=(54-58, 29-33), right=(49-53, 39-43) - right eye at initial position!
# Then LEFT, LEFT: left=(54-58, 19-23), right=(49-53, 29-33)
# Hmm, this doesn't get both eyes to initial position.
# Actually, I think the issue is that the eyes are constrained by the face shape.
# Let me just try SPACE at the current position and see what happens.
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
